analysis.device: NVIDIA RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell Server Edition

operators: aten::cudnn_convolution op_id=12 (3 kernels: 2x convertTensor + 1x cutlass tf32)

bottleneck.description: Every cudnn_convolution operator carries TWO void cudnn::engines_precompiled::convertTensor_kernel<float,...> launches immediately before its compute kernel. These are NCHW<->NHWC layout-transpose kernels. The cuDNN tensor-op convolution path the workload selects is native NHWC (the compute kernel name ends in _nhwc_align4 for the cutlass kernels, and _nhwckrsc_nhwc for the sm80_xmma kernel), but the framework tensors are laid out NCHW (default contiguous), so cuDNN inserts a transpose-in / transpose-out around every conv. The convertTensor kernels report empty ncu metrics (metrics.raw: {}) so per-kernel counters are unavailable, but their durations are directly observable: ~3000-7000 ns each, two per conv, across 6 conv operators = 12 launches in this attributed window totalling roughly 50,000 ns. These kernels do zero useful arithmetic: they exist only to reconcile the framework's NCHW layout with cuDNN's preferred NHWC layout. Annotating the model and input to torch.channels_last makes the framework tensors NHWC-contiguous, so cuDNN consumes them directly and the convertTensor transposes are eliminated. The convolution compute kernels themselves are unchanged (already NHWC tensor-op).

transformation.description: Convert the model parameters and the input tensor to torch.channels_last (NHWC) memory format before compilation. This makes the framework-side tensors NHWC-contiguous, matching the layout the cuDNN tensor-op convolution kernels already consume internally, so Inductor/cuDNN no longer need to emit convertTensor NCHW->NHWC transposes around each convolution. The intervening BatchNorm/ReLU/MaxPool triton kernels are layout-agnostic elementwise/pooling ops and propagate channels_last cleanly. As an FX-level alternative when the module dtype/layout cannot be changed at the call site, insert aten.clone with memory_format=torch.channels_last on the conv input nodes; however the module-level .to(memory_format=...) approach below is strongly preferred because it sets the layout once at the graph boundary rather than re-cloning per conv.
transformation.location: Model construction / pre-compile boundary (preferred), or aten-level FX graph at each aten.cudnn_convolution.default input node.
fx_steps: # Preferred (no FX surgery) -- set layout at the boundary before torch.compile:
# cuDNN now consumes NHWC framework tensors directly; convertTensor transposes vanish.
# Aten-level FX fallback (only if boundary layout cannot be set):
transformation.code_hint:
# Verify the transposes are gone by re-profiling and confirming no
# convertTensor_kernel<float,...> launches remain inside the conv NVTX ranges.
# channels_last is the canonical fix for NCHW<->NHWC churn around cuDNN tensor-op convs.

estimated_impact.memory_traffic_reduction: Eliminates 12 full activation read+write transpose passes. The largest conv activations are [16,128,64,64] and [16,256,32,32]; each convertTensor pass reads and writes the full tensor (the 4096x512 grid kernels move multiple MB). Removing both the in- and out-transpose per conv removes ~2x full-activation DRAM round-trips per convolution.

optimizations.notes: This is the highest-confidence, lowest-risk transform: it is layout-only, numerically exact, and targets kernels that perform no useful math. A prior optimization run on this exact workload confirmed channels_last yields the real (modest) speedup, because the conv compute kernels were already NHWC tensor-op in the baseline -- so the only recoverable waste is the transpose churn, not the convolution arithmetic. Independent of OPT-2 (no ordering constraint): OPT-2 changes dtype, OPT-1 changes layout; best applied as one combined boundary cast.

operators: aten::cudnn_convolution op_id=12 (64->128, dominant kernel k_00084 cutlass tf32, 76768 ns)
aten::cudnn_convolution op_id=33 (64->128, dominant kernel k_00103 cutlass tf32, 76896 ns)
aten::cudnn_convolution op_id=18 (128->256, dominant kernel k_00090 cutlass tf32, 68480 ns)
aten::cudnn_convolution op_id=39 (128->256, dominant kernel k_00109 cutlass tf32, 68704 ns)

bottleneck.description: The four large convolutions dominate the budget (~71% combined). Their compute kernel is cutlass_tensorop_s1688fprop_optimized_tf32_128x64_16x6_nhwc_align4. The _tf32_ token means cuDNN selected the TF32 tensor-core path: tensor cores ARE engaged (smsp__pipe_tensor_cycles_active.avg.pct_of_peak_sustained_active = 66-73%, tensor_core_active_pct 66.5/73.2) and sm_throughput is high (58-64%). So these convs are already TF32-tensor-core and roughly compute-bound -- this is NOT an idle-tensor-core situation. The remaining inefficiency is two-fold. (1) Dtype: TF32 multiply uses a 19-bit mantissa path; BF16 tensor-core throughput on Blackwell is roughly 2x the TF32 rate, and BF16 also halves the register footprint, addressing the second symptom. (2) Occupancy: achieved_occupancy is only ~8.3% with registers_per_thread=150 (150*128 threads/block = 19200 reg/block, plus 73.73KB dynamic smem/block tightly limits resident blocks per SM), and eligible_cycles_pct is ~26% -- the warp scheduler frequently has no eligible warp, so even though tensor cores are busy a meaningful fraction of cycles are lost to latency that more resident warps would hide. The lower-register BF16 tensor-op kernel raises occupancy and reclaims part of that gap. The BN+ReLU+MaxPool+mean fused triton group (15.5%) is memory-bound (dram_throughput 62-84% on its sub-kernels, tensor_core 0%); BF16 halves the bytes it moves, giving close-to-linear speedup on that DRAM-bound work.

transformation.description: Cast the convolution activation and weight operands to bfloat16 so cuDNN dispatches the BF16 tensor-op convolution path instead of the TF32 path. The cleanest inference approach is model.bfloat16() + input.bfloat16() at the boundary; the aten-level FX pass below inserts aten._to_copy casts around each cudnn_convolution (and around the BN/ReLU/pool epilogue) so the elementwise group also runs in BF16, halving its DRAM traffic. The 3->64 stage-1 convs (op_id 7/28/generic) use the sm80_xmma path with only ~16% tensor-core activity because 3 input channels under-fill the tensor-core tiles; BF16 helps them less, but their combined share is only ~10% so this is acceptable.
transformation.location: Pre-compile boundary (preferred: model.bfloat16()), or aten-level FX graph: cast operands of aten.cudnn_convolution.default and the fused BN/ReLU activations.
fx_steps: # Preferred (no FX surgery) -- combine with OPT-1 layout in one boundary cast:
# Aten-level FX fallback -- cast conv operands to bf16, restore fp32 after:
if node.op != 'call_function' or node.target is not CONV:
transformation.code_hint:
# TF32 is already in effect (the kernel name carries _tf32_), so there is no
# further TF32 knob to pull -- BF16 is the path to higher tensor-core throughput here.
# If BF16 accuracy is unacceptable, this OPT is not applicable and OPT-1 + OPT-3 carry the gains.

estimated_impact.memory_traffic_reduction: Convolutions are compute-bound so the conv gain is throughput (BF16 ~2x TF32 tensor-core rate, plus occupancy recovery from 8.3% via lower register pressure). The BN+ReLU+MaxPool+mean group is DRAM-bound at 62-84% throughput; BF16 halves its bytes for a near-linear speedup on that 15.5% slice.

optimizations.notes: Confidence is medium (not high) for two reasons: (1) the convs are already tensor-core-active in TF32, so the realized speedup depends on how much of the kernel time is genuine MMA vs. epilogue/memory and on whether cuDNN selects an equally-tiled BF16 kernel for these exact shapes; (2) BF16 changes numerics. The 150000 ns / 33% estimate assumes ~1.6x on the four big convs and ~1.7x on the DRAM-bound BN group; treat as a relative comparison only (durations are ncu-replay-inflated 2-5x). Combine with OPT-1 channels_last on the same boundary cast. Listed as prerequisite_for OPT-3 because the conv-bn fold allocates folded weight/bias buffers and must see the final runtime dtype (register_buffer-after-dtype-promotion rule); within the aten level OPT-2 must precede OPT-3.

operators: aten::_native_batch_norm_legit_no_training (fused with conv/relu/maxpool/mean across 14 triton kernels, 70496 ns, 15.5%)

bottleneck.description: In eval mode each BatchNorm2d is a fixed per-channel affine map y = gamma*(x-mean)/sqrt(var+eps) + beta. In the current graph it survives as aten._native_batch_norm_legit_no_training, lowered into the triton epilogue kernels triton_poi_fused__native_batch_norm_legit_no_training_relu_* . These kernels read the full conv output activation from DRAM, apply the affine + ReLU, and write it back -- the largest one (k_00085/k_00104 _relu_4, [16,256,32,32]-class) runs at 75% DRAM throughput for ~13000 ns. Because the BatchNorm affine is a constant-per-channel scale/shift in eval mode, it is algebraically foldable into the immediately-preceding convolution: W' = gamma/sqrt(var+eps) * W (broadcast over output channels) and bias' = beta - gamma*mean/sqrt(var+eps). After folding, the conv epilogue produces already-normalized output and the separate BN scale/shift arithmetic disappears; only ReLU (and pooling) remain as the epilogue. This removes the BN affine arithmetic that currently rides on each DRAM-bound post-conv activation pass.

transformation.description: Fold each eval-mode BatchNorm2d into its preceding Conv2d at the aten level: rewrite the convolution weight/bias from the BN running statistics (running_mean, running_var, weight=gamma, bias=beta, eps) and delete the BN node, leaving the ReLU/pool epilogue. This is the standard conv-bn fold. The pass must run after dtype promotion (OPT-2) because it allocates a new folded weight/bias buffer at the active runtime dtype -- a buffer registered before promotion cannot be recast. Because the conv has bias=False here, the fold introduces a new bias' term, so the rewritten conv must be given a bias argument.
transformation.location: Aten-level FX graph: each aten._native_batch_norm_legit_no_training.default node whose input is an aten.cudnn_convolution.default (or aten.convolution.default) output with no other consumers.
fx_steps: # Preferred: let the framework do it before compile (most robust):
# torch.fx.experimental conv-bn fuser or torch.ao fuse_modules over (conv,bn[,relu]) lists
# Aten-level FX fold sketch (runs AFTER OPT-2 dtype promotion):
if node.op != 'call_function' or node.target is not BN:
if conv.op != 'call_function' or conv.target is not CONV or len(conv.users) != 1:
# node.args: (input, weight=gamma, bias=beta, running_mean, running_var, momentum, eps)
gamma, beta, rmean, rvar = node.args[1], node.args[2], node.args[3], node.args[4]
# scale = gamma / sqrt(rvar+eps); fold into conv weight (dim0=out_ch) and bias.
# Build folded-weight / folded-bias as graph nodes or precomputed constants,
# point conv at them, then replace BN uses with the conv output.
transformation.code_hint:
# Conv weight has bias=False in this model, so the fold MUST add a bias':
#   bias' = beta - gamma * running_mean / sqrt(running_var + eps)
#   W'    = W * (gamma / sqrt(running_var + eps)).reshape(out_ch,1,1,1)
# Verify the BN running stats are constants (eval mode) before folding.

estimated_impact.memory_traffic_reduction: Removes the BN affine arithmetic from the DRAM-bound epilogue. The ReLU/pool epilogue still reads+writes the activation, so the saving is the BN scale/shift compute and any extra intermediate, not a full kernel elimination. Estimate is conservative.

optimizations.notes: Confidence is low because a prior run on this workload indicated AOTAutograd/Inductor already perform the eval-mode conv-bn affine fold to a degree -- the BN survives in the kernel NAME (triton_..._native_batch_norm_..._relu_*) but its arithmetic may already be baked into the elementwise epilogue, in which case an explicit fold is a near no-op. Verify against the Inductor-generated triton source before investing: if the kernel still issues an explicit per-channel mul+add reading running_var/running_mean, the fold helps; if it already bakes constants, skip. Must run AFTER OPT-2 (dtype promotion) per the register_buffer-after-dtype rule; ordering enforced within the aten level.

global_notes: 2 operators below the 1% threshold omitted (combined 1.37% of attributed time): aten::addmm op_id=21 (0.68%, 3104 ns, 1 kernel) and aten::addmm op_id=42 (0.69%, 3136 ns, 1 kernel). Both are the 256x10 Linear classifier head running gemmSN_TN_kernel (cublas small-N GEMM) with empty ncu metrics; at 0.7% each they are not actionable, and a 16x256x10 GEMM is far below any tensor-core tiling threshold.

fused_kernel_double_count flag ACTIVE and central to the budget. The operator list contains layer::unique::prologue (is_fused=true, 636575 ns, 55 kernels) which is the dedup REPRESENTATIVE of the entire forward pass -- it re-attributes the SAME physical cuDNN/triton kernels that are ALSO itemized under the granular aten::cudnn_convolution / aten::_native_batch_norm_legit_no_training / aten::addmm operators. Summing the prologue together with the granular operators would double-count, because dedup ran multiple forward iterations through the same compiled graph. The time_budget above is computed over the GRANULAR operators only (total_attributed_ns = 453535 ns); the prologue is intentionally EXCLUDED from the budget to avoid double counting. This matches the honest accounting convention for this workload.

total_attributed_ns = 453535 ns, summed over the 11 granular non-prologue operators that have non-null aggregated blocks. duration_ns values are 2-5x inflated by ncu application-replay counter-collection overhead -- all impact figures are relative comparisons within this profile, never absolute wall-clock predictions.

warm_up_inflation flag ACTIVE: several operator kernel_counts are not uniform multiples of a single measure_iters (e.g. aten::convolution has 4 kernels = 2 dedup iterations x 2 triton input-pack kernels, while aten::cudnn_convolution generic has 2). The dedup pipeline profiled multiple forward passes (prologue = unique representative + unrolled duplicate iterations visible as op_id=7/12/18/21 layer-B and op_id=28/33/39/42 layer-C). Per-op metrics are stable across iterations (low variance), so attribution is sound; the non-uniform counts reflect dedup unrolling, not measurement corruption.

Architecture context: hardware-limits.md not found on disk. Device NVIDIA RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell Server Edition (Sm100-class). A100 SXM5 fallback was NOT used because device_name is present; Blackwell-specific behavior assumed: warp_cycles_per_instruction is null for ALL operators (counter removed on Blackwell), so the latency-bound proxy eligible_cycles_pct < 20% is used instead. The four large convs report eligible_cycles_pct ~26% with tensor_core_active ~66-73% and sm_throughput ~58-64% -- compute/tensor-core-bound at low (~8.3%) occupancy, not latency-bound. SM count not available on disk; no wave-quantization claim is made for these convs since their grids (512-1024 CTAs) are large and they are clearly throughput-bound.

No multi_stream_overlap (all kernels stream_id=7), no cuda_graph_replay (compile_mode=dedup-inductor, not cudagraphs), no clock_domain anomaly (all timestamps ~4.0e9 ns, positive, in range), no dynamic_shapes (same-named kernels show <5% duration variance), no high_unattributed (unattributed_kernels is empty; the 18 warnings are pre-NVTX warm-up/initialization kernels k_00000-k_00017 correctly excluded by the attribution engine).

Bottleneck mechanism summary: this is NOT an idle-tensor-core workload. The dominant convolutions already run the TF32 NHWC tensor-op cuDNN path (tensor_core_active 66-73%). The recoverable waste is (a) NCHW<->NHWC layout-transpose churn around every conv (OPT-1, highest confidence, layout-only and numerically exact), (b) TF32->BF16 tensor-core throughput plus occupancy recovery from 8.3% (OPT-2, medium, changes numerics), and (c) eval-mode BN affine fold into conv weights for the DRAM-bound 15.5% epilogue group (OPT-3, low, likely partly already done by Inductor).

The 3->64 stage-1 convolutions (op_id 7/28/generic, combined ~10.5%) use sm80_xmma_fprop_implicit_gemm_..._wo_smem with only ~16% tensor-core activity because 3 input channels under-fill the tensor-core MMA tiles -- an intrinsic small-channel inefficiency with no clean FX remedy at this batch/resolution; flagged for awareness, not proposed for transformation.

Application order: OPT-1 (channels_last) and OPT-2 (BF16) are independent and best applied together as a single boundary cast model.to(memory_format=torch.channels_last).bfloat16(). OPT-3 (conv-bn fold) must run AFTER OPT-2 because it allocates folded weight/bias buffers at the runtime dtype (register_buffer-after-dtype-promotion rule). All three are aten/boundary transforms with no cross-level prerequisite cycles.

MCP tools (context7, exa-search, memory, sequential-thinking) were not invoked; FX API patterns and the fallback DAG were derived from the documented IR-level rules. Per fallback policy this caps novel transforms at medium confidence -- OPT-1 retains high confidence because it is a standard, workload-validated channels_last layout fix rather than a novel transform.
